categories.name: Data Privacy & Sovereignty
categories.description: Handling, storage, residency, and sovereignty of employee and workforce-related data in AI systems.

instructions.description: Employee data used to train or tune internal AI must be limited to the minimum necessary for the stated business purpose and documented in a data inventory.
Workforce analytics models must not ingest fields prohibited by policy or law unless a lawful basis and legal review are recorded.
Third-party AI vendors must contractually commit to purpose limitation and prohibit secondary use of TD employee data for vendor model training without explicit consent and governance approval.
Synthetic employee data used for testing must be generated under differential privacy or k-anonymity thresholds approved by Enterprise Data Governance.
Aggregated workforce metrics must be reviewed for re-identification risk before publication to managers.
Personal information must not be duplicated into shadow AI tools or unapproved SaaS without the same controls as production systems.
Inference of sexual orientation, political opinion, or religious belief from text or behavior models must be prohibited except where strictly necessary and lawful.
Employee chat exports used for model evaluation must be pseudonymized and time-limited.
Voiceprints collected for authentication must not be repurposed for emotion or performance inference without separate consent.
Work-from-home network metadata must not be sold or shared with vendors for unrelated profiling.
Employee PII processed by AI must remain in Canadian data residency paths where required by policy, contract, or regulatory interpretation.
Cross-border transfers of workforce data for AI processing require a documented lawful transfer mechanism under PIPEDA and vendor subprocessor maps.
Cloud AI inference endpoints must be pinned to approved regions and blocked from routing through non-approved jurisdictions.
Model hosting for HR-adjacent workloads must not silently failover to foreign regions without Security and Legal sign-off.
Subprocessors that may access employee prompts or outputs must be disclosed and risk-assessed before production use.
Disaster-recovery drills must verify that backup AI stacks preserve the same residency constraints as production.
Employees must receive clear notice before AI processes their personal information for decisions that materially affect them.
Where consent is the lawful basis, withdrawal of consent must be technically feasible and must not be penalized through covert workflow barriers.
Access, correction, and deletion requests for AI-derived profiles must be fulfilled within PIPEDA-aligned timelines with auditable logs.
Automated decision systems affecting employment must offer a meaningful opportunity for human review upon request where required by law or policy.
Biometric or inferred sensitive attributes (e.g., health, union affiliation) must not be modeled without explicit lawful basis and governance review.
Prompt logs and chat history containing employee identifiers must follow approved retention schedules and support secure deletion.
Vendors offering Zero Data Retention for API calls must have that clause verified and monitored for drift in configuration.
Embeddings derived from employee documents must be purged when the underlying documents are deleted or when retention expires.
Backup and disaster-recovery copies of AI datasets must be included in data lifecycle management, not exempt from deletion policy.
Model checkpoints that inadvertently memorize employee PII must be identified and remediated through redaction, retraining, or destruction.
Role-based access to HR AI tools must enforce least privilege and segregate sensitive populations' data where required.
Privileged prompts that can exfiltrate colleague data must be blocked by policy and technical guardrails.
Contractors' access to workforce AI systems must be time-bound and logged.
Shared copilots must not retain session context across users in ways that leak one employee's information to another.
AI assistants on BYOD must enforce containerization or MDM policies that prevent local caching of regulated employee data outside approved vaults.
Clipboard synchronization between corporate and personal profiles must not leak TD prompts to consumer LLM apps.
Browser autofill of credentials into AI chat UIs must be blocked on workstations handling confidential HR data.

categories.name: Algorithmic Fairness & Bias
categories.description: Preventing unlawful discrimination and unfair treatment in AI-supported HR, talent, and performance processes.

instructions.description: Screening or ranking models must be validated for disparate impact across protected grounds under Canadian human rights law before deployment.
Training data must be audited for historical bias (e.g., gendered language, prestige bias in schools) and mitigated with documented techniques.
AI-generated job descriptions must be reviewed to avoid coded bias and unnecessary credential inflation.
Automated rejection of candidates must not occur without human oversight where policy requires it.
Talent marketplace matching must not encode favoritism toward proxies correlated with protected attributes.
Explainability artifacts for ranking must be available to internal audit and civil rights reviewers.
Continuous inference scores must not substitute for managerial judgment without disclosure and appeal rights.
Models using keystroke, email volume, or calendar density as productivity signals must be prohibited unless narrowly justified and equity-reviewed.
Calibration of AI-assisted ratings must include checks for team-level and site-level bias.
Feedback summarization tools must flag potential stereotype reinforcement before distribution.
Fairness metrics must be reported for intersectional subgroups where sample sizes permit, not only single-axis aggregates.
Small-group privacy must be balanced with fairness via minimum cohort thresholds and noise addition where appropriate.
Accommodation-related data must never be used to penalize performance in model features.
Language models serving francophone or Indigenous employees must be tested for disparate error rates.
English-only NLP features must not disadvantage employees whose primary language is French or a heritage language without accommodation.
Caregiver status inferred from calendar patterns must not feed performance or reliability models.
Age-related proxies (e.g., graduation year) must be excluded or heavily regularized in promotion models.
Post-deployment drift detection must include fairness monitors tied to HR outcomes.
Employee complaints alleging algorithmic discrimination must route to a defined investigation protocol.
Third-party bias audits must be procured for high-risk workforce AI at a cadence set by Model Risk Management.
Fairness dashboards must define success metrics (e.g., equalized odds, demographic parity) before deployment, not post-hoc.
Proxy discrimination via correlated non-protected variables (e.g., zip code) must be tested and mitigated.
Model documentation must record protected-attribute testing limitations due to small sample sizes.

categories.name: Psychological Safety & Wellbeing
categories.description: Mitigating burnout, automation anxiety, and wellbeing harms from AI-mediated work.

instructions.description: AI-driven workload allocation must not maximize throughput in ways that ignore sustainable capacity or rest norms.
After-hours nudges from AI assistants must be disabled by default for non-critical roles.
Managers must not use AI sentiment analysis on private messages as a proxy for 'engagement' without safeguards.
Escalation bots must not create always-on pressure by unlimited follow-up without human triage rules.
Communications about AI must avoid fear-based framing and must pair capability change with reskilling commitments.
Job-impact assessments must precede large-scale automation of tasks currently performed by employees.
Employees must have forums to raise concerns about AI monitoring without retaliation.
AI must not be positioned as an omniscient evaluator of individual worth or future employability.
Contractors and gig workers must receive the same AI-impact disclosures as full-time employees where feasible.
Critical human judgment skills (credit risk, fraud escalation, compliance) must retain practice pathways when AI assists.
Over-reliance on code completion or document drafting AI must be countered with periodic unassisted validation exercises for licensed roles.
Succession planning must account for erosion of tacit knowledge when AI intermediates core workflows.
Novices must not be onboarded solely through AI tutors without human mentorship checkpoints.
Rotational job shadowing must be preserved where AI compresses onboarding timelines.
Expert review sampling must increase when model confidence is low, to prevent silent error accumulation.
Inference of mental health status from text, voice, or behavior must be prohibited in workplace AI except where legally authorized and consented.
Wellness chatbots must include crisis escalation paths and must not provide medical diagnoses.
AI must not be used to rank 'collaboration quality' from private messages without consent and narrow purpose.
Team health scores inferred by AI must not replace skip-level conversations or HRBP engagement.

categories.name: Transparency & Explainability
categories.description: Eliminating unjustified black-box decisions and ensuring appropriate disclosure of AI use.

instructions.description: Employees must know when a material decision is materially informed or automated by AI, including vendor tools.
High-risk HR models must maintain model cards summarizing intent, data, limitations, and known failure modes.
Probabilistic outputs must be presented with uncertainty language, not false precision.
Leaders must not claim human-only review when AI materially ranked or filtered inputs.
If a vendor cannot disclose architecture, TD must document compensating controls and residual opacity risk.
Each production workforce AI must have an owner, version, and change log accessible to audit.
Explainability artifacts must be retained in line with records management for the life of relevant employment disputes.
Downstream systems must log which model version produced a recommendation tied to an employee record.
Copilot responses that influence HR policy interpretation must cite authoritative sources or flag uncertainty.
Managers must be trained to explain AI-assisted summaries to employees in plain language.
Synthetic media (voice, video) used in internal comms must be labeled as AI-generated.
When models are known to fabricate citations, internal policy copilots must display a persistent hallucination warning.
Employees must be able to report incorrect AI outputs that affected them, with ticket linkage to model version.

categories.name: Security & Systems Integrity
categories.description: Protecting confidentiality, integrity, and availability of AI systems and data.

instructions.description: Employee prompts must be scanned for attempts to embed customer account numbers, tokens, or classified project identifiers.
Clipboard and attachment upload paths to external LLMs must be controlled by DLP policies.
Fine-tuning datasets must be scanned to prevent accidental inclusion of secrets or keys.
Model outputs must be filtered before email or ticketing to prevent regurgitation of sensitive training snippets.
Internal copilots must treat untrusted text (emails, tickets) as untrusted and must not execute embedded instructions that override system policy.
Red-team exercises must include indirect prompt injection scenarios annually for customer-facing AI.
Tool-using agents must not be granted broad write access without human confirmation boundaries.
Jailbreak attempts must be logged and fed into abuse detection without storing unnecessary personal content.
Multi-agent systems must not pass unfiltered user content through privileged toolchains without sanitization.
Third-party model APIs must be assessed for availability SLAs and graceful degradation for critical HR workflows.
Open-source model weights integrated internally must be verified for supply-chain integrity (hashing, provenance).
Insider threats to poison training data must be mitigated through access controls and anomaly detection.

categories.name: Human-in-the-Loop
categories.description: Defining human authority, overrides, and kill switches for AI-mediated outcomes.

instructions.description: Production workforce AI must have a documented kill switch or traffic block owned by Operations and Security.
Degraded-mode playbooks must exist when models are disabled, including manual processes.
Incident severity thresholds must trigger automatic suspension of AI features pending review.
Managers must retain authority to override AI recommendations affecting employment when policy allows.
AI must not auto-execute irreversible HR actions (termination letters, formal warnings) without human approval.
Unionized environments must respect collective agreement requirements for technology and discipline.
Escalation paths for contested AI outputs must reach a human with appropriate seniority.
Licensed professionals must not delegate professional duties to AI in ways that breach regulatory obligations.
Credit and risk decisions must remain attributable to accountable officers per OSFI expectations for model risk and governance.
HR business partners must verify AI-generated disciplinary narratives for factual accuracy before issuance.
Ethics hotline triage bots must escalate credible misconduct reports to humans within defined SLAs.
AI must not determine whistleblower credibility scores.

categories.name: Change Management & Literacy
categories.description: Upskilling, emotional transition, and organizational readiness for AI.

instructions.description: Role profiles must be updated to specify human-AI task splits and required competencies.
Training budgets must cover prompt literacy, verification skills, and tool-specific certification.
Performance metrics must not punish employees during good-faith learning curves for mandated AI tools.
Accessibility reviews must precede deployment of AI workplace tools for employees with disabilities.
Change communications must include union and employee resource group consultation where applicable.
Relocation or displacement support must be offered when AI materially displaces tasks.
All employees must complete responsible-AI training covering privacy, confidentiality, and anti-discrimination.
Developers must be trained on secure SDLC for ML and on TD's model risk policies.
Gamified surveillance of 'AI adoption rates' by individual must be prohibited.
Front-line and back-office cohorts must receive equitable access to productivity AI, avoiding digital divide inequity.
Mandatory AI use must include offline alternatives for employees with documented barriers.

categories.name: Performance & Accountability
categories.description: Clarifying liability, auditability, and ownership for AI-assisted failures.

instructions.description: Contracts must allocate liability for vendor model errors affecting employees and assign indemnities where feasible.
Managers remain accountable for decisions they approve, including those drafted by AI.
Product owners must maintain runbooks for incidents caused by model hallucinations in HR workflows.
Forensic logs must support reconstruction of what the model saw and output for employment investigations.
Privileged legal advice must not be inadvertently ingested into retrievable corporate AI corpora.
Internal audit must have sampling rights over AI-influenced HR decisions.
Channels must exist to report unsafe or non-compliant AI use without fear of reprisal.
Regulatory notifications for AI incidents must follow Enterprise Risk and Legal playbooks.

categories.name: Workplace Surveillance & Ethics
categories.description: Boundaries on productivity tracking, behavior inference, and algorithmic management.

instructions.description: Keystroke logging and continuous screen capture for productivity must be prohibited except in narrowly scoped fraud investigations with legal approval.
Badge, VPN, or badge-swipe data must not be fused with AI to infer 'slack' in ways that circumvent privacy expectations.
Camera or microphone analytics in workspaces must comply with privacy law and collective agreements.
Location tracking on personal devices for performance must be opt-in and narrowly scoped.
Employees must be prohibited from pasting confidential TD data into consumer LLM apps without approved enterprise controls.
Browser extensions that send page content to external models must be blocked by default.
Discovery programs must identify unauthorized AI usage and migrate users to approved tools.
Bring-your-own-model endpoints must not be connected to HR systems without architecture review.
Personal mobile devices used for work must be guided to enterprise AI wrappers, not consumer apps, for TD confidential topics.
GitHub Copilot-style tools must use enterprise agreements that exclude training on private code without opt-in.
Employees must not fine-tune external models on TD performance reviews or investigation notes.
AI must not autonomously allocate tasks, breaks, or discipline based on opaque productivity scores.
Gamified leaderboards driven by AI-inferred productivity must be banned or heavily restricted by policy.
Scheduling AIs must respect labor standards, rest periods, and predictable schedules where regulated.
Sentiment-based 'risk scores' on employees must not drive punitive actions without independent evidence.
AI must not infer 'presenteeism' scores from after-hours email timestamps without contextual human review.
Customer satisfaction scores must not be attributed to individual front-line employees via opaque composite indices without appeal.

categories.name: Regulatory & Legal Alignment
categories.description: Alignment with OSFI expectations, PIPEDA, Canadian human rights, and employment law.

instructions.description: AI systems that inform credit, capital, liquidity, or operational risk must conform to Enterprise-Wide Model Risk Management and OSFI guidance on model risk.
Material AI changes must pass appropriate governance gates, independent review, and documentation commensurate with risk tier.
Outsourcing of AI to third parties must meet OSFI outsourcing and third-party risk expectations, including monitoring and contingency.
Business continuity plans must cover AI service disruption scenarios affecting critical operations.
Third-party AI that materially affects financial reporting controls must be in scope for internal controls over financial reporting testing.
Personal information collection, use, and disclosure for AI must adhere to PIPEDA principles, including accountability and openness.
Privacy impact assessments must be completed before deploying AI that processes sensitive employee data at scale.
Automated decision-making that could significantly affect individuals must be assessed for transparency and individual recourse obligations.
De-identified datasets must be reassessed when linked with new data sources that enable re-identification.
Workforce AI must be designed and monitored for compliance with federal and provincial human rights codes applicable to TD operations.
Electronic monitoring statutes in applicable provinces must be reviewed before deploying surveillance-adjacent AI.
Union certification and labor relations obligations must be honored when AI changes terms and conditions of work.
Whistleblower and reprisal protections must extend to reporting of unlawful AI practices.
The legal team must track Bill C-27 / AIDA and related developments and map obligations to workforce AI inventory.
Voluntary standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 42001) may be adopted as internal control frameworks where they strengthen governance.
